coredata.dc:title: Quantitative detection of nitrite with N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers composite-based electrochemical sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Liu, Dong
Wang, Kun
Mao, Hanping
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we firstly report a novel N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers (NGQDs@NCNFs) composite and its application to construct highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for nitrite determination. The NGQDs@NCNFs composite was prepared by combining electrospinning, carbonization and a facile route of one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared composite possessed free-standing film structure and high flexibility, which was favorable for electrode modification. NGQDs successfully grafted on the surface of NCNFs, thus avoiding the large aggregation and maintaining the large electroactive surface area of NGQDs. While the NCNFs acted as a novel support for NGQDs attachment and benefited fast electron transfer. The electrocatalytic performance of the NGQDs@NCNFs composite was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. It is revealed that the electrochemical nitrite sensor developed based on the novel NGQDs@NCNFs composite exhibited superior analysis performance to previously reported nitrite sensors, such as wide linear range (5–300μM, R2 =0.999; 400–3000μM, R2 =0.997), low detection limit (3μM), excellent reproducibility and selectivity, and good testing recoveries for the detection of nitrite present in sausage, pickle, lake water and tap water. These results suggest that NGQDs@NCNFs composite could be a promising and convenient material for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped graphene quantum dots
N-doped carbon nanofibers
Electrochemical sensor
Nitrite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical nitrite sensor
Characterization of the as-prepared NGQDs@NCNFs composite
Electrochemical studies of NGQDs@NCNFs modified GCE
Detection of nitrite on NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical sensor
Electrochemical behavior of nitrite at different modified electrodes
Effect of scan rate on the oxidation of nitrite
Effect of buffer pH value on the oxidation of nitrite
Validation of the electrochemical sensor for detection of nitrite
Determination of nitrite in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A,D) SEM, (B,E) enlarged SEM and (C,F) TEM of NCNFs (A–C) and NGQDs@NCNFs (D–F). (G) XRD of NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs. (H) FT-IR spectra of NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs. Inset: FT-IR spectra of NGQDs.
(A) XPS spectra of NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs. (B–D) High-resolution survey spectra of N1s of NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs, respectively.
(A) CVs and (B) EIS of bare GCE and different modified GCE in 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with 0.5M KCl.
CVs of (A) bare GCE, (B) NGQDs/GCE, (C) NCNFs/GCE, (D) NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE, (E) NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE and (F) NGQDs@CNFs/GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 4.0) in the absence and presence of 1mM nitrite. Scan rate: 50mV/s.

(A, B) DPVs for nitrite in the concentrations range of 5–300μM and 400–3000μM, respectively. (C) Calibration curves of oxidation peak current vs. the concentration of nitrite under optimum conditions. (D) Effect of different interferential ions on the DPV response of nitrite oxidation.
Determination results of nitrite at various concentrations in real sample.

head.ce:title: Quantitative detection of nitrite with N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers composite-based electrochemical sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, High-tech Key Laboratory of Agricultural Equipment & Intelligentization of Jiangsu Province, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology
High-tech Key Laboratory of Agricultural Equipment & Intelligentization of Jiangsu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new electrochemical sensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers composite was firstly designed and applied for nitrite detection.

Herein, we firstly report a novel N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers (NGQDs@NCNFs) composite and its application to construct highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for nitrite determination. The NGQDs@NCNFs composite was prepared by combining electrospinning, carbonization and a facile route of one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared composite possessed free-standing film structure and high flexibility, which was favorable for electrode modification. NGQDs successfully grafted on the surface of NCNFs, thus avoiding the large aggregation and maintaining the large electroactive surface area of NGQDs. While the NCNFs acted as a novel support for NGQDs attachment and benefited fast electron transfer. The electrocatalytic performance of the NGQDs@NCNFs composite was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. It is revealed that the electrochemical nitrite sensor developed based on the novel NGQDs@NCNFs composite exhibited superior analysis performance to previously reported nitrite sensors, such as wide linear range (5–300μM, R2 =0.999; 400–3000μM, R2 =0.997), low detection limit (3μM), excellent reproducibility and selectivity, and good testing recoveries for the detection of nitrite present in sausage, pickle, lake water and tap water. These results suggest that NGQDs@NCNFs composite could be a promising and convenient material for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is one of the most widely used industrial salts, which is often served as dietary supplements and preservatives in deli, sausage and pickle. However, nitrite is highly poisonous to human and animals at high concentration [1]. Nitrite can react with amines in food and convert to carcinogenic N-nitrosamines, which probably results in stomach cancer and “Blue Baby Syndrome” [1]. Additionally, nitrite can irreversibly react with hemoglobin in human blood and produce methemoglobin, a compound which will reduce the oxygen transport capability of blood [2]. Therefore, development of new methods for highly sensitive detection of nitrite in food, water and environment has attracted considerable attention in the past decades. A variety of analytical techniques, including spectrophotometry [3–5], chromatography [6], chemiluminescence [7], electrochemiluminescence [8], biosensing method [9] and electrochemical method [10–14], have been proposed for nitrite determination. Compared to other methods, electrochemical method is more powerful due to its advantages of simple, inexpensive, sensitive, selective and fast analysis [1]. Electrochemical detection of nitrite is generally based on either reduction or oxidation of nitrite on the surface of electrode [15]. The cathodic reduction of nitrite is usually interfered from nitration ion and molecular oxygen, while anodic oxidation of nitrite can avoid the limitation [9,15]. However, the oxidation overpotential of nitrite at anode is relatively high on bare electrodes. Therefore, different electrochemical nitrite sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity based on various chemically modified electrodes (CMEs) have been fabricated recently [16–19].

Carbon-based nanomaterials have been extensively employed for the electrochemical field owing to their wide potential window, low cost, excellent chemical stability and easy renewable surface [1]. In order to solve the relatively inert electrochemical activity or poor biocompatibility of carbon-based nanomaterials, many efforts have been adopted currently [20,21]. Among them, combination of carbon-based nanomaterials with other types of nanostructures or doping heteroatoms, especially N atom into carbon nanomaterials could greatly enhance their electrocatalytical performance [22–24]. Thus, various electrochemical nitrite sensors based on composites including carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [10,18], graphene [25,26], carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [27], fullerenes [17] and porous carbons [28] have been widely developed and studied.

Recently, as a promising material in the graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), especially N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) have attracted broad interests in electrochemistry due to their unique features including large surface-volume ratio, abundant structural defects at the surfaces and edges, excellent water solubility and low toxicity [29–34]. However, the conductivity of GQDs is relatively low to some degree, which restricts the electron transfer rate and further limits electrocatalytic activity towards analytes [35]. It has been reported that using CNTs or graphene as the support for NGQDs can not only well disperse GQDs and avoid large aggregates, but also facilitate electron transfer and enhance the electrocatalytic performance of NGQDs [35–38]. However, as far as we known, using CNFs as a support to anchor NGQDs and improve the electrochemical activity of NGQDs has not been reported.

In the present work, we developed a novel all-carbon-based composite consisted of N-doped graphene quantum dots decorated on the N-doped carbon nanofibers (NGQDs@NCNFs) surface by electrospinning, post-annealing, and hydrothermal method. The construction, composition and morphology of the as-prepared NGQDs@NCNFs composite were characterized in detail. We also evaluated the electrocatalytic performance of the NGQDs@NCNFs composite by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, dopamine and [Fe(H2O)6]3+/2+ as electrochemical probes. Finally, the developed NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical sensor was applied to detect nitrite, which indicated great potential of NGQDs@NCNFs composite as a promising and convenient material for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, Mw=150, 000), Nafion solution (5wt.%), dopamine hydrochloride (DA) and ammonium iron (II) sulfate hexahydrate ([Fe(H2O)6]3+/2+) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dicyandiamide (DCD) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Citric acid (CA), dimethylfomamide (DMF), potassium ferricyanide ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−), nitrite (NaNO2), Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KCL, HClO4, ZnSO4 and other common interfering ions were procured from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). All of the reagents were of analytical grade, and used directly without any further purification.

Structure and morphology of the as-synthetized NGQDs@NCNFs composite was characterized by the means of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) operated on a PHILIPS XL-30 ESEM with an accelerating voltage of 20kV, and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operated on a TECNAI F20 field-emission TEM with an acceleration voltage of 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra was record by A D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS, Germanny) X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å). A Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument with Al Kα radiation (VG Scientific, UK) was used to survey the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) and UV–vis adsorption spectra (UV–vis) were conducted on a VERTEX 70 FTIR and a UV mini 1240 spectrophotometer, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with 0.5M KCl as supporting electrolyte on an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N workstation with a frequency range from 0.01–105 Hz.

Electrochemical experiments including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out by a CHI 832 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Apparatus Inc., China). All CV, DPV and EIS experiments were recorded by a conventional three-electrode system, where glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) modified with different material was used as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCL) and a Pt wire as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.

The NCNFs were synthesized by the combination of electrospinning and carbonization according to our previous report [39]. As shown in Scheme 1 , PAN fibers were firstly prepared by electrospinning 10wt% PAN in DMF solution under the conditions with the electric field intensity of 128kV/m and the flow rate of 1.0mL/h. Next, the electrospun PAN nanofibers were stabilized and carbonized in N2 atmosphere at 300°C for 60min and 900°C for 60min, respectively. It is notable that two combustion boats were stacked and formed an enclosed device, where N-containing tail gas could be reused for N doping of CNFs [39]. As a control, the pristine CNFs were prepared via the same route in one combustion boat, but stabilized in air atmosphere.

The NGQDs@NCNFs composite was prepared by a facile route of one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA), dicyandiamide (DCD) and NCNFs. The preparation process was shown in Scheme 1. In a typical synthesis, 100mg of CA, 50mg of DCD, 5mL of distilled water, 20mg of the as-prepared NCNFs were sealed into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180°C for 12h. The reactor was then cooled to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the obtained NGQDs aqueous solution, abbreviated as NGQDs-composite, were filtered with a 0.22μm filtering membrane, and the NGQDs@NCNFs composite was washed with ethanol and distilled water several times to remove the unreacted precursors and uncombined NGQDs. Finally, the NGQDs@NCNFs composite was dried at 60°C for 3h in a vacuum oven. For comparison, pristine NGQDs and NGQDs decorated CNFs (NGQDs@CNFs) were prepared by the same approach without NCNFs and using CNFs instead of NCNFs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical nitrite sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical sensor was fabricated as follows: first, the surface of glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm) was mechanically polished with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina powder, respectively, and then ultrasonically rinsed well with water, ethanol, acetone and water successively. Next, the NGQDs@NCNFs film with a disk shape and a diameter of 3mm was directly adhered to the dried surface of the GCE [39]. Finally, 2μL 0.5wt.% nafion was dropped on the surface of NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE and dried naturally. For comparison, NGQDs/GCE, NCNFs/GCE, NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE and NGQDs@CNFs/GCE were prepared by the similar process. Among them, the NGQDs/GCE and NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE were fabricated by casting 3μL NGQDs or the mixture of NGQDs and NCNFs aqueous solution (NGQDs-NCNFs) on the surface of GCE, respectively.

The sausage and pickle samples were purchased from a local supermarket and pretreated as follows: first, 20g samples were cut into small pieces, and then mixed with 100mL distilled water. The mixture were transferred into a juicer and squeezed into mash, followed by sonication for 30min 8mL 30% ZnSO4 (w/w) was then added to precipitate the proteins, and the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min for several times. Subsequently, the supernatant were filtered through a 0.22μm filtering membrane, and finally diluted to 200mL using phosphate buffered solution for electrochemical analysis of nitrite.

The lake water (South Lake, Changchun, China) and tap water samples were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min, and then filtered through a 0.22μm filtering membrane. Finally, the samples were diluted with a 50-fold using phosphate buffered solution for the detection of nitrite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared NGQDs@NCNFs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology and microstructure of the NGQDs@NCNFs composite were investigated by SEM and TEM. Fig. 1 A–C exhibit the SEM, the enlarged SEM and TEM images of NCNFs. It can be obviously observed that NCNFs exhibited a relatively smooth surface with the average diameter of about 350nm. After combined with NGQDs via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and dicyandiamide (DCD), the average diameter of NCNFs change unobviously (Fig. 1D). However, the enlarged SEM and TEM images of NGQDs@NCNFs (Fig. 1E and F) show that the surface of NGQDs@NCNFs composite is rougher than that of NCNFs, with NGQDs are uniformly anchored on the surface of NCNFs. The rough surface of NGQDs@NCNFs could be ascribed to the formation of NGQDs on the surface of NCNFs, and N-containing compound DCD may etch the surface of NCNFs and introduce more N into the support matrix during the hydrothermal treatment. In addition, NGQDs decorated on NCNFs exhibit larger particles size in comparison with the pristine NGQDs (Fig. S1A). But similar UV–vis absorption peaks of pristine NGQDs and NGQDs-composite at 335nm were obtained (Fig. S1B), indicating successful loading NGQDs on the surface of NCNFs.

XRD was conducted to obtain more information about the structure of the NGQDs@NCNFs composite. As can be seen from Fig. 1G, pristine NCNFs display a diffraction peak at around 21.8° with the interlayer spacing of 0.41nm, accounting for the stacked graphite layers in NCNFs. While NGQDs present a broad representative peak centered at around 22.9° with the interlayer spacing of 0.38nm. The more compact interlayer spacing in NGQDs suggests excellent crystallization of the NGQDs [29]. However, compared with individual NCNFs or NGQDs, the interlayer spacing of NGQDs@NCNFs (0.42nm) increases further. The increase in interlayer spacing is indicative of more structural defects in NGQDs@NCNFs, which suggests that larger surface area and higher electrocatalytic activity of NGQDs@NCNFs could be expected [29].

The chemical structure of NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs were also investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. For NGQDs (Insert of Fig. 1H), the characteristic absorption for stretching vibration of CO at 1658cm−1 and 1573cm−1, CN at 1387cm−1, CO at 1048cm−1 and NH or OH at 3030–3182cm−1 are observed [34]. However, after combined with NCNFs, NGQDs@NCNFs composite exhibits similar FT-IR spectrum to NCNFs (Fig. 1H), with slight red-shifted of the absorption location.

The surface composition of the as-produced NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs were further characterized by XPS. The survey spectra of the materials show three predominant peaks located at around 285.0, 400.0 and 530.8eV, assigning to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The content of C, N and O in different materials is summarized in Tables S1, from where it can be clearly seen that the doping concentration of N in NCNFs is lowest (4.28%). This should be attributed to the low level of N-containing precursor during the material preparation process. In contrast, the content of N in NGQDs (10.71%) and NGQDs@NCNFs (10.87%) increase obviously, due to the fact that N-containing compound DCD was used as nitrogen source to dope N atoms into the framework of NGQDs or NGQDs@NCNFs [40]. However, it has been demonstrated that the N content is not directly related to the electrocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon-based nanomaterials [41]. Therefore, the high-resolution N1s spectra for NGQDs, NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs were investigated in detail, and corresponding results are shown in Fig. 2B–D. For NGQDs, two peaks located at 399.5 and 400.3eV are assigned to CNC and N-(C)3, respectively (Fig. 2B) [40]. While NCNFs and NGQDs@NCNFs both exhibit three peaks, corresponding to CNC (pyridinic-N), CNC (pyrrolic-N) and N-(C)3 (graphitic-N) with binding energy at 398.7±0.3eV, 400.4±0.3eV and 401.4±0.3eV, respectively (Fig. 2C and D) [39]. The calculated percent of different type of N are listed in Table S1. Compared with NGQDs and NCNFs, NGQDs@NCNFs composite presents the lowest pyridinic-N and graphitic-N content, but the highest pyrrolic-N content (89.07%). It is worth noting that compared with pyridinic-N and graphitic-N, carbon material doped with high pyrrolic-N content at the edges of graphene layers can present better charge mobility and donor-acceptor ability [42]. Comprehensively, the NGQDs@NCNFs composite with high pyrrolic-N may display the best electrocatalytic performance relative to NGQDs and NCNFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical studies of NGQDs@NCNFs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
Using redox species [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, dopamine (DA) and [Fe(H2O)6]3+/2+ as electrochemical probes, the electrochemical performance of different materials including NGQDs solution, NCNFs film, the mixture solution of NGQDs and NCNFs (NGQDs-NCNFs), NGQDs@NCNFs film and NGQDs@CNFs film modified GCE were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A and Table S2, NGQDs/GCE (30.58μA) and NCNFs/GCE (30.08μA) exhibit comparable oxidation peak currents (ipa) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The ipa only increases slightly on NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE (32.27μA) in comparison with NGQDs/GCE or NCNFs/GCE. And the peak separation (ΔE) is still large (114mV), indicating a slower electron transfer rate of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE. However, modification of NGQDs@NCNFs onto the surface of GCE significantly increases the ipa (60.16μA) and dramatically decreases the ΔE (58mV). The excellent performance should be attributed to the large electroactive surface area of NGQDs@NCNFs according to the Randles-Sevcik equation:
(1) ipa =2.99×105 n A C D 1/2 v 1/2
where, ipa, n, A, C, D, v represent the oxidation peak current, the number of electron transfer, the electroactive surface area, the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, and the scan rate, respectively [43]. Based on the equation, the ipa is proportional to the electroactive surface area with all other factors being equal. Thus, due to the higher surface area of the composite compared with NGODs, NCNFs, and NGQD-NCNFs, the combination of NGQDs and NCNFs enhance the electron transfer kinetics of the reactant species.

In addition, the doping of N into CNFs is also contributive to produce abundant defective sites and benefit to fast electron transfer rate [39]. The conclusion can be further confirmed by the CV response on NGQDs@CNFs/GCE (Fig. 3A and Table S2), where the ipa decreases (from 60.16μA to 25.46μA) and ΔE increases (from 58mV to 85mV) upon no doping of N atom into CNFs.

For DA and [Fe(H2O)6]3+/2+, NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE also display the best electrochemical performance with larger ipa and smaller ΔE in comparison with other modified GCE (Table S2 and Fig. S2), further indicating the large surface area of NGQDs@NCNFs composite. Importantly, it is special to note that the ΔE value at NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE are 190mV- and 390mV-decrease than that at NGQDs/GCE for DA and [Fe(H2O)6]3+/2+, respectively. The results are powerful evidence that NCNFs are essential support to load NGQDs and effectively improve electron transfer rate of the composite.

EIS technique was employed to investigated the intrinsic conductivity of the as-obtained composite in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.5M KCl (Fig. 3B). As illustrated in Fig. 3B and Table S2, the NCNFs/GCE possessed relatively small resistance value (b), suggesting preferable conductivity property of NCNFs film. NGQDs/GCE exhibits the largest electron-transfer resistance of 1.614kΩ (a) due to the poor conductivity of NGQDs. While the resistance value decrease to 1.075kΩ upon mixing with NCNFs (c). The resistance of NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE further decrease (d, 0.191 kΩ) in comparison with NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE, indicating that NGQDs@NCNFs composite can form better electron transfer pathway between GCE and electrolyte than the mixture of NGQDs and NCNFs. However, the resistance increases to 0.890 kΩ again with no N-doping in CNFs (e). It is demonstrated that N plays an important role in improving the conductivity of the composite, agreeing well with results from CV measurements.

Comprehensively, these characterization and investigation demonstrate that the as-synthesized NGQDs@NCNFs composite with high pyrrolic-N content, abundant defective sites and large electroactive surface area could be an ideal material to fabricate electrochemical detection platform for sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite on NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical sensor
Electrochemical behavior of nitrite at different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic sensing behavior of the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE towards nitrite was investigated. Fig. 4 shows the typical CVs of different electrodes, including bare GCE (A), NGQDs/GCE (B), NCNFs/GCE (C), NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE (D), NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE (E) and NGQDs@CNFs/GCE (F) in 0.1M PBS solution (pH=4.0) with 1mM nitrite at a scan rate of 50mV/s. As can be seen from the results, compared with those obtained at the bare GCE (3.672μA), NGQDs/GCE (9.914μA), NCNFs/GCE (4.181μA), NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE (6.834μA) and NGQDs@CNFs/GCE (10.54μA), a remarkably larger peak current was obtained at the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE (20.56μA), which is 5.60-, 2.07-, 4.92-, 3.01- and 1.95-fold higher than other modified electrodes, respectively. It is also important to note that the oxidation overpotential of nitrite at NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE (0.822V) was 229 mV-, 168 mV-, 89 mV-, 138 mV- and 49 mV-less positive than that at the bare GC, NGQDs/GCE, NCNFs/GCE, NGQDs-NCNFs/GCE and NGQDs@CNFs/GCE.

The increased peak current and decreased oxidation overpotential of nitrite at the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE can be attributed to the factors: (i) As a novel support for NGQDs, NCNFs restrict the NGQDs from aggregation and hence, maintain the large surface area and abundant active sites of NGQDs [35,44]. (ii) Upon introducing NCNFs, the conductivity of NGQDs@NCNFs composite is sharply improved in comparison with NGQDs alone or the mixture of NGQDs and NCNFs, leading to fast electron transfer rate between modified GCE and electrolyte [45]. (iii) The NGQDs@NCNFs composite has relatively higher pyrrolic-N in their structures, thus presents better charge mobility and donor-acceptor ability. Therefore, the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE has an excellent electrocatalytic activity toward nitrite sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on the oxidation of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the reaction kinetics, the effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic oxidation of nitrite at the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE was studied. As shown in Fig. S3A, the oxidation peak current of nitrite increased with the scan rate, and a good linear relationship was obtained while plotting the peak current against square root of scan rate with the correlation coefficient value of 0.993 (Inset of Fig. S3A). The result indicates that the oxidation of nitrite is diffusion controlled process at NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of buffer pH value on the oxidation of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Apparently, the pH value of buffer solution influences the oxidation of nitrite. Thus, a series of CVs of 1mM nitrite were recorded on the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE in 0.1M PBS at different pH values (3.0-5.5). The current response of nitrite gradually increased with the pH value increased from 3.0 to 4.5, and then decreased when the pH value exceeded 4.5 (Fig. S3B). The result may be attributed to the fact that nitrite is unstable in strong acidic medium [46]. Therefore, PBS solution with pH value 4.5 was selected for the next electrochemical sensing study of nitrite in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the electrochemical sensor for detection of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique was performed to detect nitrite under the optimum conditions using the developed NGQDs@NCNFs-based electrochemical sensor. Fig. 5 A and B display the typical DPV responses of NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE toward different concentrations of nitrite. Corresponding curves show well-defined and stable anodic oxidation peak current curves for nitrite. And the oxidation current (I p) was increased linearly with the nitrite concentration (C) in two ranges of 5–300μM and 400–3000μM. The linear regression equation is I p =0.00987C − 0.0270 and I p =0.0120C+0.341 with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.999 and 0.997, respectively (Fig. 5C). The low of detection (LOD) for nitrite analysis based on the developed electrochemical sensor was determined to be 3μM. The linear range and LOD for nitrite at NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE were compared with some recently reported electrochemical sensors (Table S3). It can be seen that the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE displays a wider linear region than most of the reported sensors and comparable LOD with other sensors. Moreover, the analysis performance of other electrodes modified with NGQDs, NCNFs, NGQDs-NCNFs and NGQDs@CNFs were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. S4, the oxidation current (I p) of nitrite at all modified electrodes increased linearly with the concentration (C) of nitrite. The linear range and the LOD for nitrite analysis based on the different electrochemical sensors were shown in Table S4. It can be clearly observed that the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE displays relatively wider linear region, lower LOD, and especially higher sensitivity among the four modified electrodes. Therefore, the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE is most suitable for the detection of nitrite in real samples.

Selectivity of the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE towards the determination of nitrite was measured by testing 0.1mM nitrite from various common interferential cations such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and NH4 + and some anions including Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 − and PO4 3−. Fig. 5D illustrated that the DPV current of nitrite changed little in the presence of 100-fold excess of the above-mentioned interferents, indicating good anti-interference ability of the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE for the determination of nitrite.

In order to ascertain the reproducibility of the proposed sensor, six GCEs were modified with the NGQDs@NCNFs composite and their current responses toward the oxidation of nitrite were recoded. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the six independent measurements was 4.27%. Besides, the RSD for five parallel determinations at one NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE was tested to be 3.02%, confirming that the fabricated nitrite sensor is reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical application of the proposed method, the NGQDs@NCNFs/GCE-based electrochemical sensor was applied to test nitrite levels in real sample including sausage, pickle, lake water and tap water using a standard addition method, and the performance of analysis were summarized in Table 1 . Good recovery was obtained in the range of 90.21–107.53% with RSD less than 10%, indicating the good practical applicability of the developed sensor for nitrite analysis in the real sample.

Moreover, as can be seen from Table 1, the content of nitrite in sausage (6.36±0.14mg/kg) and pickle (28.57±1.33mg/kg) sample obtained from the proposed method was consistent well with that of the UV–vis method (6.16mg/kg for sausage and 26.95mg/kg for pickle, respectively), suggesting strong reliability of the present method.

In summary, a sensitive and selective electrochemical nitrite sensor based on the novel NGQDs@NCNFs composite was developed. NGQDs possess large effective surface area, while NCNFs can offer high electrical conductivity and free-standing film structure, which was favorable for electrode modification. Using NCNFs as a novel support of NGQDs can not only maintain the large electroactive surface area of NGQDs, but also improve the conductivity of NGQDs, leading to fast electron transfer rate. On this basis, the developed electrochemical sensor for nitrite assay displays superior analysis performance such as wide linear range (5–300μM and 400–3000μM), low detection limitation (3μM), good selectivity and excellent reproducibility, suggesting that the proposed sensor is applicable in food, environmental and other areas.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475124 and 21675065), the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (2014-37) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160490).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Libo Li obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), University of Chinese Academy of Sciences (UCAS) in 2015. Since 2016 to now, she is a lecturer at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests are focused on the preparation of functional carbon-based nanomaterials (including carbon nanodots, carbon nanofiber and grapheme) and their applications in electrochemical analysis and environment.

Dong Liu obtained his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from CIAC, UCAS in 2013. Now he is an associate professor at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interest is preparation of electrospun carbon nanofibers and their applications in electrocatalysis and energy.

Prof. Kun Wang is a professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Who received his Ph.D. from Nanjing University (2005) under the direction of Prof. Hongyuan Chen. His research interests concentrate on designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry, energy, and environment.

Prof. Hanping Mao, Professor of Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (Ministry of Education of China) in Jiangsu University, and Member of Discipline Appraisal Group of State Council. His research interests mainly include: the intelligent agricultural equipment and technology, precision applying fertilizer and pesticide, detection technology of life information, and etc.

Prof. Tianyan You received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from CIAC, UCAS in 1999. Since Dec. 2003, she has been working in CIAC as a professor. Currently, she is working in School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University as a full professor. Her research interests are focused on the functional nanomaterials and their applications in precision agriculture.
